evidence.title: Acute Otitis Externa - Clinical Practice Guideline | AAFP
evidence.snippet: Patients with AOE should be assessed for pain and recommended an analgesic treatment based on pain severity.

evidence.title: Oído de nadador: MedlinePlus enciclopedia médica
evidence.snippet: Mantenga los oídos secos y limpios, y NO permita el ingreso del agua al oído al ducharse, echarse champú o bañarse en la tina. Seque los oídos cuidadosamente después de haberse mojado. Evite nadar en aguas contaminadas. Use tapones de oídos al nadar. Pruebe mezclando 1 gota de alcohol con 1 de vinagre blanco y coloque dicha mezcla en los oídos después de que estos se humedezcan. El alcohol y el ácido del vinagre ayudan a prevenir la proliferación de bacterias. Nombres alternativos

evidence.title: Otitis Media: Diagnosis and Treatment | AAFP
evidence.snippet: Initial immediate or delayed antibiotic treatment Antibiotic treatment after 48–72 h of failure of initial antibiotic treatment Recommended first-line treatment Alternative treatment (if penicillin allergy) Recommended first-line treatment Alternative treatment Amoxicillin (80 to 90 mg/kg per day in 2 divided doses)

evidence.title: Infección crónica del oído: MedlinePlus enciclopedia médica
evidence.snippet: Su proveedor puede recetar antibióticos si la infección es causada por bacterias. Es posible que estos medicamentos deban tomarse por largo tiempo. Se pueden administrar por vía oral o en la vena (vía intravenosa ).

note_text:
S: The patient is a 45-year-old male presenting with a 4-day history of constant, intense ear pain accompanied by a little yellow, crusty discharge. He reports a recent cold last week but denies fever, chills, swelling, redness, tenderness around the ear or jaw, dizziness, severe headache, or facial weakness. He has a history of childhood ear infections and has tried acetaminophen (Tylenol) with minimal relief. There is no recent water exposure, swimming, or trauma to the ear.

O: Phone-only encounter; no physical exam or vital signs available.

A: The working diagnosis is acute otitis externa given the intense ear pain, yellow crusty discharge, and recent upper respiratory infection. 
Differential:
1. Acute otitis externa
2. Acute otitis media with tympanic membrane perforation
3. Chronic suppurative otitis media
4. Foreign body or external ear canal trauma

P: - First-line medication: topical antibiotic ear drops (e.g., ciprofloxacin or ofloxacin)
- Alternatives: topical antibiotic-steroid combination drops
- Medication safety considerations: no known allergies, no renal or liver disease, pregnancy not applicable
- Follow-up recommendations: Seek in-person medical care urgently if pain worsens, fever develops, hearing loss increases, or facial weakness occurs; seek emergency care if severe headache or dizziness develops
- Testing: Not indicated at this time; consider otoscopic examination if symptoms persist or worsen
